Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The board has two vertical "channels" in the black shape - left at col 21, right at col 36. LEFT/RIGHT moves both squares horizontally by 5 when at same row. UP/DOWN moves squares through their channels by 5 rows, but only if they're in the correct column. Current: left at (41, 21), right at (46, 36). The right square is in the right channel and needs to go UP into the orange region.
- Recent findings: Blue squares moved from row 51 to row 46 (up 5 rows again). They're moving up consistently through the black shape's "legs".
- Plan: Continue moving left square UP into yellow, then work on right square
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 71 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 2.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
RIGHT moved the right square from col 39 to col 34 (left by 5, not right!). And the left square stayed at col 19. So RIGHT moved the right square LEFT by 5 columns.
Wait, that's strange. RIGHT moved the right square from 39 to 34, which is LEFT by 5. Let me reconsider.
Actually, looking more carefully:
- Before RIGHT: left at (39, 19), right at (44, 39)
- After RIGHT: left at (39, 19), right at (44, 34)
So RIGHT moved the right square from col 39 to col 34 (left by 5). The left square didn't move.
This is confusing. Let me think about what's happening:
- LEFT moves both squares left by 5 (when they're at the same row)
- RIGHT moves only the right square left by 5??
Wait, maybe LEFT/RIGHT don't move "left" and "right" in the traditional sense. Maybe they're controlling different squares:
- LEFT controls the left square
- RIGHT controls the right square
But earlier, LEFT moved both squares. Let me check the history more carefully.
Actually, looking at the ascii:
Row 44: `BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOSSSSSBBBBBBBBBBB`
- The right blue square is now at displayed cols 24-28, which is actual cols 34-38
- It's now adjacent to the orange region (OO at cols 32-33)
So RIGHT moved the right square to be next to the orange region. Maybe the goal is to get each blue square next to its corresponding colored region?
- Left square should be next to yellow
- Right square should be next to orange
But the left square is already surrounded by yellow on its right side (cols 24-28 are YYYYYY).
Wait, let me reconsider. Looking at the row 44:
`BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBYYYOOSSSSSBBBBBBBBBBB`
- Cols 10-28: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB (black)
- Cols 29-31: YYY (yellow)
- Cols 32-33: OO (orange)
- Cols 34-38: SSSSS (blue - right square)
- Cols 39-49: BBBBBBBBBBB (black)
The right square is now at cols 34-38, with orange (OO) at cols 32-33 to its left. 
Hmm, but the left square is at cols 19-23, and to its right (cols 24-28) are YYYYYY (yellow). So the left square is already adjacent to yellow.
Maybe the goal is to get both squares adjacent to their colored regions? The left is already adjacent to yellow, and the right is now adjacent to orange.
But wait, the right square was moved LEFT by RIGHT. That's counterintuitive. Let me think about this differently.
Maybe LEFT/RIGHT don't control direction but rather control which square:
- LEFT = move left square
- RIGHT = move right square
And the movement direction depends on something else?
Or maybe the squares move toward the center? The left square is at col 19 and the right at col 34. The center is around col 26-27. So:
- LEFT moves the left square right (toward center)
- RIGHT moves the right square left (toward center)
That would make sense! Both squares move toward the center.
But earlier, LEFT moved both squares left. Let me check the history again...
Actually, I don't have the full history in this turn. Let me just focus on what I know now:
- Left square at (39, 19) - can't move UP (blocked by black)
- Right square at (44, 34) - adjacent to orange on the left
Let me try UP on the right square now that it's at col 34. Or maybe I should try UP on the left square again.
Actually, looking at the board structure again:
- Row 38, cols 19-23: BBBBB (black) - blocks left square from going UP
- Row 43, cols 34-38: OOOOO (orange) - above the right square
If I move the right square UP, it would go into the orange region. Let me try UP.
But wait, the left square is also present. Will UP move both or just one?
Let me try UP and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model update:
- RIGHT moved the right square from col 39 to col 34 (left by 5, toward center). Left square stayed at col 19.
- RIGHT appears to move the right square toward the center. The right square is now adjacent to orange (OO at cols 32-33).
- Left square at (39, 19) is blocked from going UP by black cells above.
- Right square at (44, 34) has orange above it at row 43.
Let me try UP to see if the right square can now move into the orange region.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try UP to see if right square moves into orange
result = action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
blues = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'S']
positions = [(n['boundary'], n['pixels']) for n in blues]
print(f"After UP: {positions}")

# Check ascii around new positions
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
for r in range(34, 54):
    if r < len(ascii_lines):
        print(f"Row {r}: {ascii_lines[r][10:50]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
